questions.question: What does the Society of Minds protocol have N model instances do?
options: Independently propose answers, then over R rounds read and critique each other's proposals until they converge
questions.explanation: N proposers, R rounds, cross-critique, convergence is the canonical Du et al. debate.

questions.question: Why does cross-model debate (e.g. ChatGPT + Bard) outperform single-model debate?
options: Heterogeneity reduces shared blind spots so cross-critique catches more errors
questions.explanation: Mixing models brings independent error distributions, which raises the ceiling of debate.

questions.question: What is the main token-cost win of a sparse topology over full mesh?
options: Each debater reads only a subset of peers, so critique-op count drops while accuracy often matches
questions.explanation: Sparse (star, ring, hub-and-spoke) reduces critique ops without losing accuracy on many tasks.

questions.question: Which case does the lesson list as where debate hurts?
options: A simple factual lookup, because one lookup is cheaper than five debates
questions.explanation: Latency- and cost-sensitive trivial lookups do not benefit from N x R debate.

questions.question: What is convergence collapse?
options: All agents converge on the first wrong answer; mitigate with required disagreement rounds
questions.explanation: Early agreement on a wrong answer is mitigated by forcing distinct round-1 proposals.

questions.question: Why does prompt homogenization undermine debate?
options: Identical prompts produce nearly identical answers, removing the cross-critique signal
questions.explanation: Diverse prompts (and ideally diverse models) keep the proposal distribution wide.

questions.question: Which production pattern is a debate variant per the lesson?
options: Anthropic orchestrator-workers with a synthesis step
questions.explanation: Orchestrator-workers with synthesis is a debate-shaped pattern in production.
